sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rules 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
sources.note: Public domain. Comma, semicolon, period rules grounded in Rules 2-6.

sources.name: Purdue OWL — Grammar and Punctuation
sources.note: Punctuation as structural tool, not decorative pause marker.

sources.name: Plain Language Act 2010
sources.note: Punctuation conventions for plain language public documents.

_description: Each punctuation mark has a defined structural role. Punctuation is not decorative. It is not a pause marker. Every mark signals a specific relationship between the elements it separates or connects.

marks.function: Ends a complete thought. Creates emphasis. The strongest pause.

rules.rule: Use a period to end every complete sentence.
Do not use a period to break a sentence fragment away from its parent sentence.
rules.example_correct: He left quickly, taking nothing with him.
rules.example_incorrect: He left quickly. Taking nothing with him.

rules.rule: A short sentence ended with a period creates emphasis. Use this deliberately.
rules.example_correct: The bridge is out. Do not cross.
rules.note: The period after a short declarative sentence is a tool for impact — not just a grammatical requirement.

marks.function: Separates actor from consequence. Separates list items. Sets off parenthetic expressions. Precedes coordinating conjunctions between independent clauses.

rules.rule: Use a comma in a series of three or more items — including before the final conjunction.
rules.example_correct: Bring your ID, your permit, and your vehicle registration.
rules.example_incorrect: Bring your ID, your permit and your vehicle registration.

rules.rule: Use a comma before a coordinating conjunction joining two independent clauses.
rules.example_correct: The gate is locked, but the side entrance is open.
rules.example_incorrect: The gate is locked but the side entrance is open.

rules.rule: Use commas on both sides of a parenthetic expression. Never one without the other.
rules.example_correct: The convoy, delayed by weather, arrived at 0800.
rules.example_incorrect: The convoy, delayed by weather arrived at 0800.

rules.rule: Use a comma after an introductory clause or phrase.
Do not use a comma to join two independent clauses without a conjunction — comma splice.
rules.example_correct: The road is closed. Expect delays.
rules.example_incorrect: The road is closed, expect delays.

marks.function: Joins two related independent clauses without a conjunction. Stronger than a comma. Weaker than a period.

rules.rule: Use a semicolon between two independent clauses that are closely related and have no conjunction.
rules.example_correct: The northern route is faster; the southern route is safer.
rules.example_incorrect: The northern route is faster, the southern route is safer.

rules.rule: Do not use a semicolon before a coordinating conjunction. Use a comma.
rules.example_correct: The road is open, but conditions are dangerous.
rules.example_incorrect: The road is open; but conditions are dangerous.

rules.rule: Use a semicolon between items in a series when the items themselves contain commas.
rules.example_correct: Teams are stationed in Beirut, Lebanon; Kabul, Afghanistan; and Nairobi, Kenya.
rules.example_incorrect: Teams are stationed in Beirut, Lebanon, Kabul, Afghanistan, and Nairobi, Kenya.

marks.function: Introduces what the preceding clause earns. The clause before the colon must be a complete independent clause. The colon points forward.

rules.rule: Use a colon to introduce a list, explanation, or quotation when the preceding clause is complete.
rules.example_correct: Three items are required: an ID, a permit, and a vehicle pass.
rules.example_incorrect: You will need: an ID, a permit, and a vehicle pass.
rules.note: You will need is not a complete independent clause. The colon is not justified here.

rules.rule: The colon must be earned by the clause before it. Do not use a colon after a verb or preposition.
rules.example_correct: The requirements are clear: all vehicles must be registered.
rules.example_incorrect: The requirements include: registration, insurance, and a permit.

marks.function: Sets off a strong parenthetic break or amplifies what follows. More emphatic than commas. Use sparingly.

rules.rule: Use an em dash to set off a parenthetic expression that requires stronger emphasis than commas provide.
rules.example_correct: The only safe route — the coastal road — is now closed.
rules.note: Use commas for routine parenthetic expressions. Reserve dashes for high-emphasis breaks.

rules.rule: Do not use a dash as a substitute for a comma, colon, or period.
rules.example_incorrect: We need three things — ID, permit, and a vehicle pass — before we can proceed — please bring them tomorrow.

blocked_uses.note: Punctuation signals grammatical and logical relationships — not breathing pauses. Do not insert commas where you would pause when speaking.
Every mark must perform a structural function. A mark that does not separate, join, introduce, or emphasize is a violation.
Exclamation marks are not permitted in any DSCL domain except direct speech in creative or journalistic contexts. Emphasis is created through sentence structure and word choice — not punctuation.
